coredata.dc:title: Preparation and optimization of grafted hydroxyethyl cellulose, polypyrrole, and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots bionanocomposites for electrical, optical, and photoluminescence multicoloring applications

coredata.dc:description: The major objective of this research revolves around the integration of polypyrrole (PPy) and various concentrations of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) into a polyacrylamide (PAm)-grafted hydroxyethyl cellulose (gHEC) to produce gHEC@PPy@N-CQDs bionanocomposites that possess environmentally sustainable properties. The intercalation and uniform distribution of N-CQDs inside the gHEC@PPy matrix have been demonstrated through the analysis of data obtained from X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). The samples underwent analysis using thermogravimetric analysis (TGA and DTG) as well as scanning and transmission electron microscopy. The improved dispersion of PPy and 4 % N-CQDs inside the matrix led to enhanced electrical characteristics of the graphene-hybridized metal bionanocomposite. The peculiar optical and photoluminescence emission observed in the gHEC@PPy@N-CQDs bionanocomposites can be attributed to the surface groups of N-CQDs and the transition between CN and CN. This hypothesis suggests that these factors play a significant role in determining the observed optical properties. The main goal is to identify distinctive and captivating applications for these bionanocomposites across several domains, including electronics, optical and light-emitting devices with a broad spectrum of colors, and bioimaging applications.

head.ce:title: Preparation and optimization of grafted hydroxyethyl cellulose, polypyrrole, and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots bionanocomposites for electrical, optical, and photoluminescence multicoloring applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The major objective of this research revolves around the integration of polypyrrole (PPy) and various concentrations of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) into a polyacrylamide (PAm)-grafted hydroxyethyl cellulose (gHEC) to produce gHEC@PPy@N-CQDs bionanocomposites that possess environmentally sustainable properties. The intercalation and uniform distribution of N-CQDs inside the gHEC@PPy matrix have been demonstrated through the analysis of data obtained from X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). The samples underwent analysis using thermogravimetric analysis (TGA and DTG) as well as scanning and transmission electron microscopy. The improved dispersion of PPy and 4 % N-CQDs inside the matrix led to enhanced electrical characteristics of the graphene-hybridized metal bionanocomposite. The peculiar optical and photoluminescence emission observed in the gHEC@PPy@N-CQDs bionanocomposites can be attributed to the surface groups of N-CQDs and the transition between CN and CN. This hypothesis suggests that these factors play a significant role in determining the observed optical properties. The main goal is to identify distinctive and captivating applications for these bionanocomposites across several domains, including electronics, optical and light-emitting devices with a broad spectrum of colors, and bioimaging applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The growing global need for an environmentally friendly and sustainable economy has had a significant impact on a range of industries, particularly the fields of nanomaterials and the science of materials. The field of green synthesis of nanomaterials has recently gained attention as a subject of research to develop materials and nanomaterials that possess qualities such as reliability, sustainability, and environmental friendliness. Nanotechnology has the potential to surpass the limitations of traditional methods by prioritizing a sustainable approach, thus contributing to the development of a more sustainable and secure future [1].

The light-emitting carbon dots (CDs) with quantum size have garnered significant interest due to their distinct physical characteristics. The range of applications in nanobiotechnology is extensive and encompasses countless other possibilities [2–4]. Following the initial research on the fabrication of CDs using laser ablation [5], numerous top-down methodologies have been suggested for the production of fluorescent carbon quantum dots. However, these approaches entail a nonselective exfoliation process, which may necessitate the application of hazardous reagents as well as specialized tools [6–8]. Produce waste, which poses environmental risks, serves as a highly plentiful and important resource for the production of eco-friendly products across diverse uses. In recent times, there has been a heightened attempt to utilize organic material due to the increased demand for recycling [9,10].

Most of these synthesis methods, however, involve several stages and the use of strong acids. Their water solubility and luminescence can be improved through post-dealing with surface passivating compounds [8]. In recent times, significant endeavors have been undertaken to achieve the one-stage synthesis of self-passivated carbon dots. Several systems have been created for the producing of amino-functionalized fluorescent nanoparticles of carbon. Hydrothermal carbonizing of chitosan at very high temperatures is one example of these processes [11], carbonization of glucose in reverse micelles and hydrothermal carbonization of sucrose with the aid of a microwave [12]. These procedures offer simple alternatives to surface passivation for the synthesis of luminous carbon nanoparticles.

The high energy density and large surface areas of the nano-ingredient contribute to the improvement of interfacial bonding and stress transmission between the polymer matrix and the nanoenhancers [13,14]. In order to fulfill practical requirements, contemporary polymer composites must integrate characteristics such as electromagnetic shielding, energy storage, electrical conductivity, and similar attributes.

Hydroxyethyl cellulose (HEC) is a commonly utilized cellulose derivative in various industries, including cosmetics, pharmaceuticals, and coatings [15]. The materials developed by the HEC should possess a high level of ultraviolet (UV) shielding capability when exposed to external conditions. However, it should be noted that the mechanical and UV-protecting capabilities of HEC exhibit a moderately low level of strength. The addition of cellulose nanofibers, cellulose nanocrystals, and inorganic nanomaterials enhances the mechanical characteristics of hydroxyethyl cellulose composites [16]. However, there is currently no known research on the simultaneous enhancement of the electrical, optical, and photoluminescence capabilities of HEC using multifunctional nanomaterials.

This work is about how to effectively use polyacrylamide grafted-hydroxyethyl cellulose (gHEC) incorporated with PPy and N-CQD. In the present experiment, our hypothesis focused on evaluating the effectiveness of the obtained materials. To reach our goal, we used a number of different techniques for characterizing the material, such as X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), morphological analysis, thermal stability testing, and checks of its electrical, optical, and photoluminescence properties for perspective applications.

Mature beech pinewood sawdust (MBs) as a waste from the carpentry workshop was air-dried and then ground and sieved using a mesh size of 450 μm. Hydroxyethyl cellulose (HEC), acrylamide (Am), pyrrole (Py), sodium dodecyl benzene sulfonate (DBSA), potassium persulfate (KPS), and methylene bis-acrylamide (MBAm) obtained from Sigma Aldrich.

N-CQDs were manufactured from MBs by combining 0.03 g of MBs with 0.07 g of NaOH and 2.4 g of urea. Then freeze the mixture for one night to create a homogenous solution. Each dissolved mixture was defrosted and ultrasonicated for 2 min. The sonicated solution was microwaved for around 7 min. in a domestic microwave (700 W). The mixture changed into a solid yellow-brown crude N-CQDs [17].

Pyrrole (10 g) and DBSA (25 g) were dissolved in 200 mL of water at room temperature under continuous stirring. After that, a solution of FeCl3.6H2O (18 g/100 ml) was added dropwise to start the oxidation polymerization at 5 to 7 °C. Polypyrrole was rinsed with distilled water and ethanol then filtrated and dried at 45 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of grafted hydroxyethyl cellulose (gHEC; A1)
ce:section.ce:para: HEC was dissolved in water to prepare a solution of 1 %. After that 0.1 g of KPS was added to the previous solution under stirring at 300 rpm and 70 °C to great active center, then 3 g of Am and 0.02 g MBAm were added to start the grafting on HEC chains. The reaction solution was dried using a freeze-dryer to get gHEC.

The previous experiment steps were performed but in the presence of 0.1 g of PPy and various concentrations of N-CQDs (0, 0.02, 0.04, and 0.08 g) to obtain A2, A3, A4, and A5 bionanocomposite, respectively, as shown in Fig. 1 .
Fourier-transform infrared spectra were collected employing a Mattson 5000 spectrometer (Unicam, United Kingdom).

The crystallinity was studied on X-ray powder diffraction as the diffraction patterns were measured by Bruker D-8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (Germany) applying a 40 kV voltage and a 40 mA current employing copper (Kα) radiation (1.5406 Å). Where the crystallinity index percent was calculated by the following Eq. (1)

Where Sc = area of the crystalline domain, St = area of the total domain, ΔCr·I (%) = change of crystallinity.

The morphological properties of N-CQDs were demonstrated by using a transmission electron microscope (TEM; model JEM-1230, Japan) that was operated at 120 kV, with a maximum magnification of 600 × 103 and a resolution of 0.2 nm. Also, the Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, Quanta 250 FEG FEI) with an EDX detector was used to study the surface morphology of as-prepared samples.

TGA analysis was performed on the prepared polymer powder employing a Perkin Elmer thermogravimetric analyzer by heating the sample to 1000 °C at a rate of 10 °C/min under the N2 atmosphere. The thermal analysis data were recorded to determine the activation energy (Ea) of the thermal degradation. The Eqs. (1) and (2) were applied under the Coats-Redfern approach.

Where n is the order of degradation reaction, α is the fractional conversion, β (K/min) is the heating rate, T (K) is the temperature, R (kJ/mol.K) is the gas constant, A (s−1) is the frequency factor and E is the activation energy. According to the Eqs. (2) and (3), plotting a relationship between (log10 [1-(1-α)1-n] / T2 (1-n)) and 1/T employing different appropriate n values should present a straight-line correlation. Therefore, the least square approach was applied by taking different n values (from 0 to 3.0), estimating the standard error (SE), and calculating the correlation coefficient (r) for each value of n. The activation energy was calculated from the slope (E/2.303R), whereas the frequency factor A was calculated from the intercept (log AR/ßE) of the Coats-Redfern equation by the most appropriate value of n. The other kinetic parameters, including enthalpy ( ∆ H), entropy ( ∆ S) and free energy change ( ∆ G) were estimated employing Eq. (4).

The dielectric measurements and AC conductivity were obtained using the Novocontrol high-resolution alpha analyzer equipped with Quatro temperature controllers.
The UV–Vis spectrophotometer measured the UV–Vis absorption spectra from 190 to 1000 nm (Jasco V-630, Japan).
Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were collected using a Spectrofluorometer model FS5 from Eidenburg – UK.

The TEM image of N-CQDs under investigation showed that it has a spherical morphology and an average size of around 8.77 nm. These N-CQDs are embedded within graphene oxide (GO) sheets. The presence of a color gradient around the borders of the sheet provides evidence for the layered structure of GO, which is composed of many stacked sheets [18], as depicted in Fig. 2 .

The FTIR spectra of as-prepared N–CQDs, gHEC (A1), and gHEC@PPy (A2) as well as their bionanocomposites with 0.02, 0.04, and 0.08 g of N-CQDs (A3, A4, and A5, respectively) were shown in Fig. 3 . The FTIR spectrum of N-CQDs revealed absorption bands at 3471, 3347, 1674, 1619, 1591,1437, 1213, 1153, 1045, and 617 cm−1 which attributed to NH, OH, CO, amide I, amide II bands, CC, O–C=O, C–O–C, CN, and C-C-N respectively. The presence of N–functional groups (i.e. NH and CN) proves the preparation of N–CQDs [17]. The FTIR spectrum of gHEC showed broad bands at 3178 and 3454 cm−1 where these peaks refer to stretching vibrations of the hydroxyl and amide groups, respectively. The CH stretching vibration showed a band at 2867 cm−1. While the bands at 1195 and 1099 cm−1 are attributed to the bands of the carbonyl group of polyacrylamide and CO stretching vibrations in the hydroglucose units of hydroxyethyl cellulose, respectively, and the band at 617 cm−1 is related to C-C-N as displayed in Fig.3. The stretching vibration absorption peak at 1675 cm−1 was attributed to the -C=O group in the Am [19,20]. These patterns authorized it to be proved that N-CQDs fillers were physically well dispersed into the prepared bionanocomposites A3, A4, and A5 as shown in Fig. 3.

The XRD spectrum indicates the presence of distinct peaks corresponding to GO at specific angles, specifically 2θ = 10.0° and 24.63°, which can be attributed to the (001) and (002) crystallographic planes of GO, respectively. This result provides evidence for the inclusion of GO within the composition of N-CQDs that were synthesized using a microwave method [21]. The crystallinity of the produced N-CQDs is confirmed by the XRD spectrum depicted in Fig. 4 . The observed peaks at 2θ = 21.86, 28.33, 30.99, and 38.93° are correspond to the crystal planes denoted as (002), (100), (102), and (103), respectively. Among these, the angles of 21.86, 28.33, and 30.99° are indicative of graphite (sp2) structure, while the angle of 38.93° corresponds to the diamond (sp3) structure. The N-CQDs that were produced exhibited a calculated CrI percentage of 60.13 %.

The XRD pattern of both the HEC and the gHEC (A1) was displayed in Fig. 4. The graph presented provides convincing evidence of a noticeable shift in the peak detected in HEC at 2θ angles of 9° and 21.5° [22], which has been altered to 19.79° following the grafting process using acrylamide, as elucidated in schematic design Fig. 1. The alteration of the polymeric backbone by morphological modification is the underlying cause of this phenomenon. The HEC undergoes crystallization upon the addition of KPS and Am in grafting polymerization process, leading to the detection of several diffraction angles (2θ) at 31.32°, 38.22°, 44.4°, 64.71°, and 77.74° in gHEC, which are not observed in HEC [23,24].

The XRD spectra in Fig. 4 illustrate the structural properties of the synergistic effects of PPy on gHEC. The XRD investigation revealed the existence of PPy within the gHEC matrix, as depicted in Fig. 4 (A2). The XRD peaks of PPy were observed within the range of 15–27°, with positions at 2θ = 21.94° and 25.13° [25]. These peaks indicate a d-space value of 4.052, representing the distance between adjacent pyrrole rings [26]. In addition, the main peaks of gHEC at 2θ = 31.32°, 38.22°, 44.4°, 64.71°, and 77.74° were shifting to lower values at 2θ = 30.52°, 38.08°, 44.34°, 64.62°, and 77.69°. That may be due to the synergistic effects of PPy on gHEC via the formation of hydrogen bonds between hydroxyl groups of the HEC glucose ring and amide groups of pyrroles [27,28]. This data matches with those that other researchers have obtained [29–31].

The XRD spectra of PPy and N-CQDs effects on the gHEC samples labeled as A3, A4, and A5 are shown in Fig. 4. Bionanocomposites A3 and A4 showed diffraction peaks at 2 theta = 21.88, 25.92, and 27.34, whereas the other diffraction peaks disappeared. This may indicate the good compatibility and miscibility between gHEC, PPy, and N-CQDs at concentrations of 2 % and 4 % in samples A3 and A4, respectively [28]. In addition, this indicates the insertion of the polymers between GO nanosheets which are included within the composition of N-CQDs [32]. Furthermore, the pure graphite has a diffraction peak at an angle of 2θ = 26.6° which can be explained by the fact that the (002) crystal plane of graphene is indexed. The complete participation of HEC in the graphite polymerization process leads to the elimination of its primary semi-crystalline structure, resulting in the formation of an amorphous shape [19].

Whilst, the XRD patterns of the bionanocomposite A5 demonstrate the successful formation of the composite, as seen by the presence of diffraction peaks associated with GO and PPy. The observed diffraction peak was ascribed to an augmentation in the stacking of uniform platelets with higher loadings of N-CQDs (8 %), as the accessible volume of the PPy matrix for expansion remained constant. Moreover, the position of the prominent peak of the polymer shifts when the weight percentage of N-CQDs in gHEC@PPy hybrids rises. Evidence of π-π stacking interactions between the PPy chains and N-CQDs sheets is observed, suggesting the potential for intermolecular interaction between the two components as shown in Fig. 4 [19]. The XRD results demonstrate that the N-CQDs were efficiently integrated and uniformly dispersed within the gHEC@PPy matrix.

Fig. 5 (A1) displays the FESEM of the gHEC image, illustrating an extended microsphere formation due to a certain cause with aggregations. The phenomenon in consideration is attributed to the modification of the polymeric backbone through morphological alterations, as confirmed by XRD data. The results obtained from the EDX analysis of gHEC indicated that the samples were composed of carbon and oxygen as their main constituents. Additionally, tiny quantities of sodium (Na), sulphur (S), and potassium (K) were detected, which can be attributed to the polymerization process of gHEC.

Upon the introduction of PPy into gHEC, the surface undergoes a transformation characterized by increased roughness. This is visually evident by the formation of tiny spherical nanoparticles with small aggregations, as depicted in Fig. 5 (A2). The elemental composition of the gHEC-PPy sample, as determined by EDX analysis, revealed that carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen were the primary elements present as depicted in Fig. 6 .

The pictures in Fig. 5 (A3, A4, and A5) show that adding N-CQDs to the gHEC-PPy matrix changes the shape of the particles' surfaces, creating network-like structures that look like groups of tree branches and nanospheres. EDX analysis of the N-CQD inserts into the gHEC-PPy matrix samples showed that the elements were carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen, with different amounts of each. The EDX results revealed that the N-CQDs had well-dispersed elements throughout the gHEC-PPy matrix (Fig. 6).

Under the N2 condition, the TGA analysis was applied to as-prepared samples to evaluate their relative long-term stability, and the segmental mobility of the produced bionanocomposites in addition to the Coats-Redfern equations were used to calculate the kinetics [33]. Fig. 7 displays the TGA curves and their derivatives (DTG), plus the DSC and the kinetics of the decomposition processes of the prepared samples. Whereas Table 1 summarizes the findings gained from this analysis. Bionanocomposite A5 (8% N-CQDs) exhibited greater stability and reduced segmental mobility compared to bionanocomposite A3 (2% N-CQDs) and A4 (4% N-CQDs), but these two bionanocomposites exhibited similar behavior in general. Weight losses of 91.57, 68.05, 50.97, 54.81, and 20.14 % were observed in TGA/DTG analyses of samples A1, A2, A3, A4, and A5, respectively, indicating the presence of non-volatile components.

The decomposition curves of sample A1 (gHEC) revealed four decomposition steps. The 1st decomposition stage occurred between 41 and 196 °C, with an extreme of 150. °C, and mass loss (ML%) of 10.00 %, which was due to the loss of moisture content. The 2nd and 3rd main decomposition steps were between 206 and 233 & 246–271 °C, with a highest at 217 & 257 °C, ML 4.04 & 15.17 %, releated to the decomposition of terminal group as ethyl (-C2H5) and amide (-CONH2) as shown in Fig. 7a [34]. The Ea for the 3rd decomposition step (21.49 kJ mol−1) was higher than the 2nd one (19.70 kJ mol−1), so it is suggested that this step is related to the decomposition of amide groups of Am (ML ≈ 15.17 %) [35]. The 4th stage was due to the decomposition of the main bone of HEC chains that was between 314 and 438 °C, with a top value at 374 °C and ML 62.36 %. The 4th step showed an intense peak in the DTG curve with ML ≈ 62.36 [17].

At the same time, the decomposition curves of sample A2 (gHEC@PPy) revealed four decomposition steps. The 1st decomposition stage occurred between 40.90 and 223.48 °C, with a maximum of 154.82 °C, and ML of 7.97 %, which was due to the loss of moisture content. The 2nd and the split 3rd main decomposition steps were between 224.12 and 255.89 and 258.02–273.61 & 275.55–336.66 °C, with a maximum of 232.26 and 262.99 & 295.76 °C and ML11.58, 14.13 & 11.82 %, attributed to the depolymerizations of organic matter (Fig. 7b). The 4th decomposition step was between 336.75 and 474.29 °C, with a maximum of 381.61 °C and ML 22.55 %, ascribed to the combustion process.

The prepared bionanocomposite A3 (2% N-CQDs) and A4 (4% N-CQDs) slightly showed the same decomposition steps. The 1st decomposition stage occurred between 44.16 and 206.76 and 44.25–180.89 °C, with a maximum of 123.91 and 142.80 °C, and ML of 7.53 and 6.33 % for 2 % N-CQDs and 4 % N-CQDs, respectively, which was due to the loss of moisture content. The 2nd and 3rd decomposition steps for A3 were between 207.11 and 233.07 and 234.58–295.35 °C, with a maximum of 227.67 and 264.39 °C, ML 0.64 and 3.12 % (Fig. 6c). The 2nd and 3rd steps for A4 were between 192.28 and 214.11 and 215.33–288.02 °C, with a maximum of 205.89 and 242.12 °C, and ML of 2.53 and 8.60 %, respectively (Fig. 7d). These 2nd & 3rd steps for A3 and A4 were attributed to the depolymerizations of organic matter. The final 4th step for A2 and A4 was between 299.20 and 479.07 and 299.20–501.71 °C, with a maximum of 366.18 and 372.02 °C and ML 40.32 and 37.35 %, recognized to the combustion process.

The bionanocomposite A5 (8% N-CQDs) decomposition curves revealed three decomposition steps. The 1st decomposition step occurred between 44.21 and 218.013 °C, with a maximum of 183.98 °C, and ML of 1.49 %, which was due to the loss of moisture content. The 2nd main decomposition step was between 244.38 and 335.28 °C, with a maximum of 296.66 °C, ML 5.68 %, ascribed to the depolymerizations (Fig. 7e). The 3rd decomposition step was between 335.13 and 466.21 °C, with a maximum of 386.57 °C and ML 12.97 %, recognized to the combustion process.

From the previous results, the bionanocomposite A5 has a lower total ML (20.14 %) compared to A3 (50.97 %) and A4 (54.81 %), which suggests that the A5 needs more temperature to degrade. The bionanocomposite A5 is more thermal stable due to the high content of N-CQDs. Consequently, it needs a high temperature to deteriorate. According to Table 2 , the values of ∆S are negative, indicating that the system degradation is non-spontaneous [36,37].

DSC testing was performed on the produced samples (Fig. 7). The crystallization and melting behaviors of the produced films were demonstrated to be significantly affected by the N-CQDs concentration. Three melting temperatures (Tm) are listed in Table 1 for the samples A1 and A2. These are labeled Tm1, Tm2, and Tm3. The high stability of the material is attested to the bionanocomposite nature of the gHEC, N-CQDs, and PPy, which was seen by the greater glass transition temperature (Tg) of the A4 and A5 films in comparison to the Tg of the pure gHEC film (A1). The analysis of the 2 % N-CQDs film using TGA didn't reveal a Tg suggesting the materials may not exhibit this specific thermal property. The crystallization peak (TP) of N-CQDs nanoparticles corresponds to an exothermic peak at 341.41 °C for a A4 film. When 8 % N-CQDs were used, the film showed three exothermic peaks at 384.69 % (TP1), 412.41 % (TP2), and 421.95 % (TP3). Two minor peaks, TP2 and TP3, are attributed to the phase change of N-CQDs nanoparticles from a cubic to a wurtzite structure, while the largest peak, TP1, represents crystallization. These two peaks may not have been observed in the A3 and A4 bionanocomposites films because of their very low content of N-CQDs.

Fig. 8 (a) illustrates the range of frequencies from 0.1 Hz to 100 MHz at which the dielectric constant was measured in various samples, demonstrating the observed variance in its value. The dielectric constant has a marginal decline within the frequency range of 0.1 Hz to 1 kHz, but it remains relatively constant for frequencies over 1 kHz. All of these individuals possess knowledge regarding the phenomenon that the dielectric constant exhibits a gradual decrease as the frequency increases. This particular attribute is universally observed among all dielectric materials. The increased permittivity observed in the low-frequency area can be attributed to the presence of several types of polarization, including dipole, atomic, ionic, and electronic polarization, among others, in these components [40,41]. The observed behavior suggests the presence of charged entities (such as grain boundary impact and oxygen shortage) that exhibit an inability to react to changes in the applied fluctuating electric field. The relative permittivity at high frequencies exhibits a decreasing trend and eventually reaches a plateau, indicating the existence of charged entities.

However, the dielectric constant was found to be relatively high at low frequencies. This is because a potential barrier is created by the presence of space charge polarization at the grain boundaries. This triggered charge accumulation at the grain boundary, which in turn boosted actual permittivity values [41]. Grain boundaries are likely more important than the grains themselves in explaining dielectric dispersion. Koop's phenomenological explanation [41,42] suggests that this is due to Maxwell-Wagner-type interfacial polarizations.

The bionanocomposite film A4 has a dielectric constant that is significantly higher than that of the other films. Changes in dielectric behavior are caused by changes in the structure characteristics of the sample as validated by XRD and thermal analysis, as well as the fact that π-electrons in the linked double bond of the polypyrrole framework can move freely between adjacent carbon atoms at this N-CQD ratio (more dispersion). This makes it easy for charges to move within the disorganized area ordered into the gHEC@PPy polymer chain, which leads to more dipole polarization.

When the polarization reverses direction behind an applied electric field because of grain boundaries, an energy loss known as dielectric loss occurs. The dielectric loss of dielectric materials typically comes from three sources: the motion of molecular dipoles (dipole loss), direct current conduction, and space charge migration [40]. Fig. 8(c) depicts the frequency dependence of the dielectric loss tangent at various samples. Permittivity's imaginary components follow the curve's pattern of behavior. Since grain boundaries have a higher resistivity than the grains themselves, they are responsible for the high value at low frequencies. The loss tangent in Fig. 8(c illustrates the frequency dependency of the samples to provide a thorough understanding of the relaxation processes. The loss tangent, is correlated with the dielectric relaxation. Dielectric loss is a phenomenon that occurs when a material's polarization moves behind the applied electric field as a result of grain boundaries. The phenomena of peak frequency shifting are found in samples that are clearly visible in A4 indicating a change in relaxation time, the peak passes through a transition towards higher frequencies, which contributes to a reduction in the amount of time spent relaxing when the ratio reaches 4 % of N-CQDs [43].

The correlation between conductivity and frequency at room temperature is depicted in Fig. 8d for gHEC@PPy with various concentrations of N-CQD. When the frequency is decreased, a greater amount of charge will collect at the interface between the electrode and the sample, which will result in arise in the conductivity of the samples that are being measured. This results in a decrease in the total number of mobile ions, which in turn results in a reduction in conductivity at low frequencies. The increased mobility of charge carriers in the material at higher frequencies is the basis of an increase in the conductivity of the material [44]. Electrical conductivity increased because to the greater dispersion of PPy and 4 % N-CQDs in the gHEC matrix. This was induced by the incorporation of sample structural characteristics and π-electrons into the connected double bond. This occurred when both PPy and 4 % N-CQDs were present.

Fig. 8 illustrates the fluctuating frequency at a temperature of 300 K. The parameters are related to separate parameters, and it is possible to verify the electrical homogeneity of substances when the peaks approach just one frequency at the same time. As a consequence of this, samples A4 and A5 exhibit the same peak at the same frequency, which verifies the homogeneity of these materials' electronic microstructures and indicates that only grain boundary contributions are responsible for the conduction process.

The diffuse reflectance spectrum (DRS) of gHEC@PPy with different amounts of N-CQD is shown as a function of wavelength in Fig. 10 a. As demonstrated, the reflection values tend to fluctuate with the inclusion of different N-CQD ratios. The reflectance value at 4 % N-CQDs in the gHEC@PPy matrix shows an enhancement, which can be attributed to the alterations in the structural properties of the sample, as confirmed by XRD and thermal analysis. A high reflectance value suggests that the thin film samples exhibit limited light penetration and performance [44]. This observation implies that the incorporation of a substantial proportion of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQD) into polymeric thick matrices confers a notable ability to prevent the absorption of radiation throughout the visible spectrum region. A major step forward in the creation of cutting-edge optical tools was achieved when it became clear that the reflectance of gHEC@PPy could be modified and synchronized by changing process conditions and adding levels of the N-CQDs contents. This insight marked a major advancement in creating the next generation of optical equipment. To calculate the energy of the gap in the optical band, the Kubelka-Munk function is applied [27]:
(9)

where KM(R) is the reflectance coefficient and R is the reflectivity.

The absorption coefficient spectra displayed in the inset in Fig. 10b predominantly exhibit two-peak features in the range of 200–500 nm. The less pronounced shoulder observed in the UV–vis absorption spectra of all the obtained samples corresponds to the π-π* transition band associated with aromatic CC bonds, as illustrated in Fig. 10b. UV–vis spectra revealed that as the ratio of N-CQD blended in gHEC@PPy rises and the center of the shoulder moves towards a larger wavelength, the shoulder from the
π −
π ∗
alteration band becomes more noticeable. Similar to this, at around 214–220, 275, and 383 nm, respectively, it was possible to identify the π −
π ∗
transition band of aromatic CC bonds, the n −
π ∗

transition of CO bonds or even other surface groups, and a broad shoulder peak caused by the existence of lower energy states because of the existence of N-related functional-surface groups as illustrated in Fig. 10. Moreover, a second absorption band was found in the ultraviolet spectra at 240–250 nm. This peak denotes the formation of a third n −
π ∗
transition band in the structure of N-CQD blended in gHEC@PPy, which managed to the production of a distinct energy state [46,47]. Its broad peak displayed a blue shift as the N-CQD variable concentrations were greatly raised.

It has been discovered that as the concentration of N-CQD in the films rises, the refractive index also rises, signifying a greater density. The aforementioned phenomenon results in a notable decrease in the velocity at which light propagates through thin films composed of gHEC@PPy@N-CQD bionanocomposites. The observed increase in refractive index values in the gHEC@PPy@N-CQD at 4 % bionanocomposite can potentially be associated with the aggregation of N-CQDs, leading to the formation of bigger clusters.

Fig. 11 depicts the correlation between the square of the product of KM (R) and hν and the photon energy hν. Based on observations, the band gap energy values are determined by extrapolating the straight line of the curve to zero along the hν axis. The energy band gap values for the bionanocomposite materials denoted as A1, A2, A3, A4, and A5, are estimated to be within the ranges of (2.19–2.16) eV, (2.77–3.23) eV, 3.72 eV, 4.14 eV, and 3.17 eV, respectively. The band gap energy of the gHEC@PPy@N-CQD framework exhibits a significantly high value. The observed phenomenon can be ascribed to the suggested alteration of the band gap of N-CQD, leading to a displacement of the Fermi level within the gap. This shift is primarily influenced by the quantum confinement effect and structural conditions [48]. The diverse ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra observed can be attributed to the distinct frameworks and compositions present.

We performed a detailed PL study at the excitation wavelength of 300 nm to evaluate the luminescence properties of the gHEC@PPy with varying amounts of N-CQD, as shown in Fig. 12 . These materials' size and surface chemical groups need to be carefully adjusted to make them luminous. Sp2/Sp3 carbon, oxygen/nitrogen-based groups, and post-modified chemical groups have always been included in luminescent carbon materials that have been created [32]. Interestingly, the amalgamations of N-CQDs into the gHEC@PPy matrixes made a blue shift towards the lower wavelength with the same intensity as a result of fabricated substances as shown in Fig. 12.

Regarding conceptual understanding, it can be observed that colloidal N-CQDs exhibit photoluminescence (PL) behavior that is reliant on their particle size, specifically when the size is smaller than the exciton radius. This behavior can be attributed to the energy gap transition of conjugated π-domains within the core, which is created using sp2 carbon [49,50]. Nevertheless, in most cases, the PL spectra of colloidal N-CQDs show a minor influence from quantum size effects, but they do display an important dependence on their chemical structures and compositions [51]. This observation highlights the intricate nature of the surface state. The surface state of N-CQDs can be described as a combination of interconnected functional groups and carbon backbones. The state being referred to in this context is separate and distinguishable from both the carbon core state and molecular fluorescence. Both the dimensions of the electron system and the chemical composition of the surface have an impact on the energy gap of the surface state [52,53]. Consequently, the presence of surface imperfections in colloidal quantum dots (N-CQDs), such as sp3 and sp2 hybridized carbons, can serve as an effective method for capturing certain entities.

In addition, the near-infrared (NIR) spectra of the gHEC@PPy with different quantities of N-CQDs demonstrate an NIR emission peak ranging from 832 to 829 nm when excited with a wavelength of 300 nm. This is seen in Fig. 12, where the inset provides a visual representation of the observed phenomenon. Luminescence imaging in the near-infrared (NIR) spectral range, spanning from 700 to 1700 nm, holds considerable importance for in vivo studies. This is primarily due to the benefits of deep tissue penetration and reduced auto-fluorescence. Consequently, there is a need to develop luminophores that possess absorption and emission properties within the NIR region [54]. The utilization of graphited HEC as a polymer matrix in this unique approach represents a noteworthy advancement in the field of materials science. Furthermore, the incorporation of PPy and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) enhances the luminescent properties of the resulting composite, enabling near-infrared (NIR) imaging capabilities [55].
Fig. 13 (A1) exhibits the Gaussian deconcoluted PL emission spectrum of gHEC and red lines representing total combined fitting to the data at 567.32, 587.02, 621.35, 646.22, and 696.27 nm.

The entire carbonization process leads to the presence of numerous oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface, including epoxide, hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxyl, and sulfoxide [56]. The functional groups mentioned are connected to carbon backbones, facilitating the solubility of N-CQDs in polar solvents. Additionally, these functional groups introduce surface defects with distinct energy levels [56,57], resulting in diverse electronic transition probabilities and a broad spectrum of PL properties for N-CQDs. Hu et al. (2019) successfully generated a range of colloidal quantum dots (N-CQDs) with tunable PL emission spanning the whole visible spectrum. This achievement was accomplished with straightforward chemical alterations and adjustments in the synthesis conditions [58]. The researchers postulated that the presence of surface epoxides or hydroxyls might potentially induce substantial local deformation, leading to the creation of new energy levels across n-π* gaps. This phenomenon would subsequently give rise to a wide range of excitation energies and PL that is dependent on the wavelength of excitation. So, the emission bands that detected the sample gHEC (A1) in the visible zone from the green to the red region as a result of polyacrylamide grafted hydroxyethyl cellulose include the hydroxyl, amide, and carbonyl groups that were validated by FTIR data.

The PL emission peaks of PPy effects on gHEC (A2) are lower and shifted to lower wavelengths at 533.66, 554.31, 298.1, 637.41, and 688.46 nm. Since nitrogen and carbon have the same electronic structure and atom radius, nitrogen-related functional groups such as amino, amidic, pyridinic, pyrrolic, graphitic, nitro, amidine, etc. [57,58] can be incorporated into both the carbon core and the amorphous exterior. Because these groups make it more likely for electrons to transition from the ground to the lowest excited singlet state, the luminescence properties of N-CQDs that are connected to surface states are greatly boosted.

As demonstrated in Fig. 13, the effects of N-CQDs on the gHEC@PPy matrix (A3, A4, and A5) Gaussian deconcoluted PL emission bands are going to the lower wavelength and contributing as visibly blue shifts due to the influence of N-CQDs at (485.66, 560.17, 607.73, and 633.26 nm for A3 in the visible area from the blue to the orange and region). The primary amines were discovered to operate as surface passivation participants, trapping surface electrons for improved radiative recombination and significantly increasing the fluorescence of the N-CQDs. Subsequently, the production of highly efficient blue-emitting N-CQDs in a normal microwave (700 W) experiment revealed the crucial role of both amino and pyrrolic N in enhancing luminescence properties. The process facilitates radiative recombination by generating additional protonation processes or aromatic frameworks [59]. Due to the wide size distribution of the sp2 domains in CQD, the band gaps of the various sp2 sizes cover a wide range, resulting in a broad PL emission.

The synthesized N-CQDs incorporated in the gHEC@PPy matrix have tunable luminescence; they are more suited for multicolor imaging as shown in Fig. 14 . To investigate the potential color emission point lying on the visible gamut's horseshoe curve, the emission spectra of the gHEC@PPy with fluctuating quantities of N-CQD have been interpreted into the chromaticity coordinates of the International Commission on Illumination (CIE). The calculated values of correlated color temperature (CCT) and chromaticity coordinate color. N-CQD was added to gHEC@PPy to fine-tune the luminous color shown in Fig. 14.

In contrast, the chromaticity visualization demonstrates that the color coordinates for A1, A2, A3, A4, and A5 are approximately in greater proximity to white chromaticity, specifically daylight, as depicted in Fig. 14. The results of this study indicate that bionanocomposites have the potential to be controlled in a way that enables them to emit light in multiple colors, spanning from red to orange, yellow, green, and even white. This characteristic holds significant relevance in the development of multicolor lighting systems, particularly those utilizing near ultraviolet-based light-emitting diodes [48,60].

The work specifically focuses on the efficient application of hydroxyethyl cellulose (HEC) that has undergone grafting and stabilizing processes, such as those using PPy and N-CQDs. The XRD, FTIR, and FESEM analyses demonstrate the efficient intercalation and uniform distribution of PPy and N-CQDs within gHEC. The TGA-DSC results show good thermal stability with the addition of N-CQDs to the gHEC@PPy. The improved scattering of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) within graphene hydrogel-encapsulated polypyrrole (gHEC@PPy) at a concentration of 4 % led to a significant improvement in the material's electrical characteristics. The distinctive optical and photoluminescence emission observed in this research is thought to be caused by the surface functional groups of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) within the graphene-enhanced polymer gHEC@PPy composite matrix, as well as the electronic transition between carbon‑nitrogen (CN) and carbon‑nitrogen double bond (C=N) configurations. The experimental results have confirmed the previous notion that bionanocomposites have advantageous dispersion, hence improving the performance characteristics of the materials. These applications encompass the development of light-emitting systems with a wide range of colors and the facilitation of near-infrared bioimaging.
